extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML (including internal block diagrams and state machine diagrams) for structural and behavioral models, complemented by architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML IBDs to capture component‑connector architecture. Methodologies referenced include RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT for managing abstraction layers. Simulation and analysis are performed with MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools, while executable code may be generated in C++ or Java. These heterogeneous models are integrated through a digital‑shadow reference model expressed as a UML class diagram.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation comprises SysML models that capture requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers, complemented by architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc which define components, interfaces, and connectors. Behavioral aspects are modeled with state‑machine specifications in SysML or MontiArc Automaton, and detailed dynamic simulations are realized in MathWorks Simulink (white‑box, physics‑based). Data‑driven black‑box artefacts, e.g., machine‑learning models, are integrated to capture non‑linear effects. Data sources consist of contextual data traces collected from the physical asset and aggregated for a specific purpose, forming the digital shadow. Model relationships are established through model‑to‑model transformations (e.g., SysML to Simulink) and through the co‑existence of physics‑based and data‑driven models within the same twin. Data management involves aggregation, abstraction, and traceability of these traces within the twin’s repository.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is provided by a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor that drives the robot axis. The motor is commanded through a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, which offers remote control interfaces for trajectory and force commands. The controller implements the necessary safety constraints and limits the actuation range according to the motor specifications, enabling precise positioning of the axis under supervisory control from the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Each drive incorporates two position encoders – a motor‑mounted encoder and a secondary encoder – delivering high‑resolution angle measurements. The sensing infrastructure provides time‑series access to all relevant signals, including input current and output angle, enabling the collection of continuous sensor data for model calibration and validation. Data are transmitted at the native sampling rates of the encoders for real‑time processing within the Digital Twin ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by mixing physics‑based (white‑box) models (e.g., Simulink) with data‑driven (black‑box) models and by validating the combined Digital Twin against measured data traces to quantify deviation. The documents stress traceability of how data are generated and verification of model transformations (e.g., SysML → simulation models). Uncertainty sources such as model abstraction, sensor noise, and transformation errors are addressed through systematic verification methods and traceability links. Quality assurance relies on model‑based development practices that guarantee correctness of transformations and consistency between the digital shadow and the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system implements an automated one‑way data flow from the physical object to a digital object, wherein contextual data traces are collected, aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose. The digital shadow is defined as a set of such data traces and their aggregation, but the document does not specify concrete data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data‑quality measures, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: According to the digital‑shadow reference model, the virtual entity is passive and does not issue commands to the physical system; therefore there is no virtual‑to‑physical interaction. The flow is strictly one‑way from physical to digital, and no control messages, validation, or feedback loops are defined.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The documents describe a MBSE‑driven lifecycle that is split into four abstraction levels: (1) requirements modeling, (2) functional architecture, (3) logical architecture, and (4) product/technical layer. Each level is represented with appropriate engineering models (e.g., SysML requirement diagrams, SysML internal block diagrams, ADL component‑connector diagrams, and implementation artefacts such as Simulink models or source code). Transition between the levels is performed by mapping concrete elements of a higher‑level model to the next‑level model, following the conformance relations defined by the reference model. Governance is implied by the need to keep the different stakeholder views consistent across the abstraction hierarchy.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the digital twin follows the MBSE workflow described in Document 1, employing methods such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to structure the development. Models are created with SysML, ACME, AADL, or MontiArc and enriched with simulation (Simulink) or code (C++, Java). Concretization of a digital shadow (the basis for the twin) is performed as a mapping from the reference‑model concepts to concrete classes, as explained in Document 2, establishing a conformance relation that can be versioned. Evolution of the twin is managed by updating the concrete realization and its traceability links, although explicit version‑control or QA practices are not detailed in the documents.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and accurately reflects the detailed modeling background provided in Section II.A and the digital shadow definition in II.B. It correctly lists the modeling languages (SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc), simulation tools (Simulink), and the distinction between white-box and black-box models. It also accurately describes the data sources as 'contextual data traces' and their aggregation. Supported by quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'architecture description languages (ADLs)... ACME, AADL, MontiArc', 'a digital shadow... a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose'.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'permanent magnet synchronous motor' and the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' controller. However, it makes several unsupported or contradictory claims. It states the controller 'offers remote control interfaces for trajectory and force commands,' whereas the text only lists 'Position, speed, or acceleration' as inputs. It claims range limits are based on 'motor specifications,' but the text attributes this to the 'safety cell of the robot.' Most significantly, it claims precise positioning is under 'supervisory control from the Digital Twin,' which directly contradicts the source's definition of a digital shadow as a 'passive entity' that 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.'

The extraction accurately describes the sensing setup, mentioning 'two position encoders per drive' (motor-mounted and secondary) and their role in reducing backlash. It correctly identifies the data types ('input current and the output angle') and the time-series access. However, it adds unsupported technical details such as 'high-resolution angle measurements' and transmission at 'native sampling rates,' which are not explicitly stated in the text.

The extraction is well-supported regarding the mixing of physics-based (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) models to improve accuracy. It correctly identifies validation via the 'deviation of simulated data versus measured data' and the importance of traceability ('trace a data trace back to its generating processing component'). It slightly extrapolates regarding 'verification of model transformations' and 'systematic verification methods' for transformation errors, as the text focuses on traceability to ensure consistency rather than explicit verification algorithms. Quotes: 'white-box and black-box friction models can be effectively combined', 'solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'traceability... trace a data trace back to its generating processing component'.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the one-way data flow definition but inaccurately claims the document does not specify 'concrete data types, communication protocols'. Section IV.A explicitly states that sensor data is 'streamed via MQTT', and Section IV.B specifies that data traces store 'an array of torque τ and angular velocity ω as floating point values'. conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

Therefore, the specific detailed characteristics requested are not present.
